doom.driver: At least 400 dead in drug rehabilitation center strike in Kabul, Afghan officials say. Afghanistan says Pakistan is to blame for a strike on a drug rehabilitation center in Kabul that killed at least 400 people, the deadliest attack since fighting began weeks ago.
doom.blurb: SECOND WAR OPENS WHILE NOBODY WAS WATCHING
doom.summary: While everyone's eyes were glued to the Strait of Hormuz, Pakistan apparently bombed a drug rehab center in Kabul and killed at least 400 people — the deadliest single strike in the region in years. A Pakistan-Afghanistan hot war quietly igniting alongside the Iran-U.S. conflict is the kind of multi-front chaos that historically ends badly, given both countries have nuclear weapons and one of them just massacred hundreds of civilians at dinner. Meanwhile, Iran launched retaliatory strikes after Israel killed its intelligence minister — the third high-profile assassination in two days — so the original crisis is also somehow getting worse.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: Pakistan-Afghanistan military conflict just produced 400 civilian deaths in a single strike while the Iran-U.S. war simultaneously escalates with retaliatory missile attacks on U.S. assets.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: Pentagon planning to train AI models on classified military data, and cheap Iranian drones are overwhelming expensive U.S. interceptors — the asymmetric warfare math is breaking down in real time.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: Top U.S. counterterrorism official resigned citing an illegitimate war started 'due to pressure from Israel,' while Gabbard faces congressional questions with no good answers.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: DOJ says Anthropic 'can't be trusted with warfighting systems' — a bureaucratic spat, but a preview of who actually controls military AI decisions.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Iran is now successfully acting as Hormuz gatekeeper with countries striking deals around the U.S., suggesting American coercive leverage is eroding faster than anticipated.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, wide aerial view looking down at 45-degree angle — Kabul sprawled across a high-altitude valley basin, the Hindu Kush mountains ringing the horizon as jagged black silhouettes against a sky drowned in blood orange and volcanic ash, their peaks swallowed by churning smoke columns that rise from the city below. The scene is dominated by a walled compound in the center of the frame — a low-roofed rehabilitation center building reduced to collapsed concrete slabs and exposed rebar, the structure's courtyard cracked open, rubble fanning outward in pixelated gray chunks across dusty brown earth. Tiny figures — no larger than two or three pixels — scatter at the compound's edges, dwarfed entirely by the devastation surrounding them. The city beyond spreads in dense amber and ochre blocks, flat-roofed buildings stretching toward mountain foothills, power lines sagging between structures. A deep volcanic underlighting bleeds upward through the smoke, casting everything in smoldering blood orange, cool blue tones almost entirely extinguished — only faint traces surviving in distant mountain shadows. The atmosphere itself feels heavy and poisoned, dust clouds diffusing the light into a sickly amber haze that presses down over the entire valley. No gore, no weapons, no named figures — only scale, ruin, and atmosphere. 16-bit SNES pixel art, no text, no antialiasing.

dawn.driver: He survived 48 hours without lungs and lived. A critically ill 33-year-old man survived an almost unimaginable scenario—living for 48 hours without lungs—thanks to a groundbreaking surgical approach.
dawn.summary: In the middle of a week dominated by airstrikes, assassinations, and energy crises, a 33-year-old man lived for 48 hours without lungs — and walked away. Surgeons developed a technique that kept him alive while his body fought a devastating flu-triggered infection, then successfully restored him. It's a quiet, almost surreal counterpoint to a world on fire: in a hospital somewhere, a team of humans refused to accept the limit.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: A man surviving 48 hours without lungs represents a frontier-crossing surgical achievement that redefines what human medicine can do.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: India's cheap semaglutide generics entering the market on March 20 signals a potential global coalition moment in the obesity and diabetes fight.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: UK dads going viral for gathering at pubs to learn how to braid their daughters' hair is a small, genuine burst of warmth amid relentless darkness.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: South-east Asian nations racing to conserve energy, however urgency-driven, is nudging governments toward reduced fossil fuel dependence faster than policy ever did.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: New research suggesting daily multivitamins may slow biological aging offers a simple, accessible tool that could extend healthy years for millions.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Weather-dominated scene, thick golden morning fog rolling through a hospital corridor at Northwestern University, warm haze diffusing the blazing overhead surgical lights into soft amber halos, the mist catching rays of early daylight pushing through large ICU windows, visibility reduced to glowing pools of gold and green. Within this atmospheric murk, a state-of-the-art thoracic surgery suite materializes — gleaming stainless steel surfaces dissolving into the fog, banks of monitors radiating electric green vital sign waves, a stabilizing heartbeat line climbing from jagged chaos to a calm steady rhythm. At the scene's luminous center, an ECMO artificial lung machine pulses with vivid red oxygenated blood cycling visibly through clear transparent tubing, the circuits looping through the golden haze like a river of life made visible. Beside the machine, the unmistakable negative space of an open chest cavity — lungs simply absent — surrounded by a masked surgical team of five figures rendered in careful silhouette against the amber surgical spotlights. In the foreground, resting on a sterile surface, bright fresh pink donor lungs glow against the white like a sunrise through the fog, contrasting sharply with the dark, damaged tissue beside them. Color palette: warm golden-yellow, fresh clinical green, deep amber fog, vivid red blood circuitry.
